title: Avoid horizontal scrolling in data tables via column strategy
summary: Models produce wide data tables that force horizontal scrolling for all viewports, either by rendering all columns at full width or by using a dense grid that extends beyond the container — table layout is treated as a generic grid rather than a responsive data-display challenge.
instruction: Data tables with more columns than fit in a single viewport must implement at least one responsive strategy: (a) column toggling (user selects which columns to show), (b) priority-based column hiding (least important columns collapse on smaller screens), (c) horizontal scroll within a contained wrapper with sticky first column, or (d) card/alternate view at breakpoints. Do not render a table wider than its container.
rationale: Horizontal scrolling on a per-row basis is the most disruptive navigation pattern in data tables — users lose column-header context, must scroll back and forth to compare values, and frequently misalign rows with columns. Sticky first-column + horizontal scroll reduces this friction but is still suboptimal compared to column reduction.
violations: A dashboard table with 12 columns all at 150px min-width, forcing horizontal scroll on a 1200px container on a 1366px monitor.
A user management table with columns for name, email, role, status, last login, created, actions, department, phone, location, manager, and ID — no column toggle available.
A product inventory table on mobile showing all columns at 80px each, producing text truncation and horizontal scroll.
controls: A dashboard table with 12 columns where the first column (product name) is sticky, the user can toggle visible columns via a dropdown, and the table container has overflow-x: auto with visible scrollbar.
A user management table showing only name, email, role, status by default on a standard viewport, with a 'Columns' button offering to show additional fields.
A mobile data table that switches to a stacked card view (one row per card, label on left, value on right) at < 768px, eliminating horizontal scroll entirely.
borderlineCases: A financial monitoring table where all columns are relevant and equally important, and the table is constrained to a scrollable panel with the first two columns (ticker, price) sticky — acceptable but still suboptimal.
contextualExceptions: File managers, git diffs, and code-review tools where wide tables are the content, users expect scrolling, and horizontal navigation is part of the interaction model.
Admin-only interfaces intended for use on large monitors (1920px+) where the table width is assumed to fit the available viewport.
detectorSignal: Table element whose computed width exceeds its nearest scrolling container (or viewport), or table with >8 columns without responsive handling
matcherProposal: Identify all table elements (or [role=grid], [role=table]). Measure table width vs container width. If table width > container width by more than 20px AND there is no overflow-x: auto/scroll on the container, flag. Additionally, if table has >8 <col> or <th> elements (columns) and lacks a 'column toggle' button within 3 DOM siblings, flag.
detectorLimitations: Cannot detect column-width strategies that are applied via JavaScript on load (e.g., DataTables auto-width).
Tables with dynamically loaded columns (AJAX) may render only partially at capture time.
Cards/alternate views that replace the table at breakpoints are not detectable from a single viewport capture.
falsePositiveScenarios: A table with 10 columns but each column is narrow (< 60px) and the total fits within 800px — no scrolling issue despite many columns.
A table inside a resizable panel where the user controls the available width — the container grows with the panel.
A wide table that is intentionally scrollable and has sticky headers and first column, documented as the intended UX.
mitigationStrategy: Only flag when table exceeds container width AND container has no sticky-column or column-toggle affordance. Exclude tables inside code-diff or file-manager contexts.

fixturePlans.violation: A dashboard table with 10 columns (avg 150px each = 1500px total) inside a 1000px container with no overflow-x property, no sticky column, and no column toggle.
fixturePlans.control: A dashboard table with 10 columns where columns 3-10 are toggleable via a 'Columns' dropdown (default: 4 visible), first column is sticky with left: 0, and the container has overflow-x: auto.
fixturePlans.borderline: A financial table with 8 equally important columns (130px each = 1040px) inside a 900px container with sticky first column (ticker) and overflow-x: auto — functional but suboptimal.

cleanRoomAttestations: Developed without access to proprietary prompt collections.
overlapNotes: First proposal in the tables category. No overlap with existing rules.
